Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Hadewijch

13th c.

Mystical desire, interior transformation, and the soul’s union with love.

A preliminary correction: Hadewijch is conventionally treated as a thirteenth-century Brabantine woman, probably connected with the beguine or mulieres religiosae milieu. I will use she/her. Her name is often expanded as “Hadewijch of Antwerp,” but the “of Antwerp” designation comes from later transmission rather than from firm biographical evidence. Veerle Fraeters summarizes the historical baseline well: Hadewijch was active around 1240, wrote in Middle Dutch, and produced letters, visions, songs, and rhymed epistolary poems centered on minne, mystical Love, understood as a path toward deification.

The report below reconstructs a “philosophy of mind” from Hadewijch’s writings. This is necessarily reconstructive: she did not write a scholastic treatise De anima or a modern philosophy-of-mind monograph. But it is not artificial. Her letters and visions contain a dense psychology of soul, reason, memory, will, affect, vision, divine cognition, and angelic mediation. Van Mierlo’s Dutch commentary even calls Letter IV “deeply psychological” and Letter XVIII is repeatedly treated in Dutch and French reception as a kind of compact mystical psychology.

The shortest thesis is this: for Hadewijch, the human mind is the soul as a God-directed, Trinitarianly structured, affectively intelligent, deifiable subject. It is not primarily an isolated inner container of representations. It is a living capacity for reciprocal vision with God, ordered by reason, driven beyond itself by Love, stabilized by memory, transformed through will, tested in embodied Christic suffering, and sometimes instructed through angelic vision. God and angels are not decorative theological additions to this account; they are part of its noetic architecture.

1. Corpus and methodological frame

Hadewijch’s works matter for philosophy of mind because she writes across several genres that each expose a different layer of mental life. The prose letters offer direct instruction in discernment, reason, will, memory, charity, and spiritual discipline. The visions stage extraordinary modes of cognition: angel-guided symbolic seeing, direct divine encounter, and non-discursive fruition. The songs dramatize the inner life of the lover-soul by means of courtly, allegorical, and affective figures such as Reason, Freedom, Prowess, and Minne. Fraeters notes that forty-five songs survive; in them, Minne is personified as the Lady, and allegorical characters such as Reason, Prowess, and Freedom help the knightly lover-soul on its quest.

Her letters are especially important. Fraeters describes the collection as thirty-one prose letters including personal exhortations, spiritual instruction, and treatises on themes such as intra-Trinitarian love and the simultaneous living of Christ’s divine and human nature; she also notes their roots in Cistercian and Victorine contemplative traditions and their Trinitarian and apophatic elements.

The visions are equally central. Fraeters describes fourteen chapters containing twelve visionary raptures, organized not simply by liturgical chronology but by Hadewijch’s exemplary growth toward perfection. The first vision places her in relation to the throne angels, while the last culminates among the seraphim, where she is shown that her soul has become perfect Minne. This matters philosophically because Hadewijch’s account of mind is developmental: the soul is not merely a faculty-bundle but a subject undergoing formation toward Godlikeness.

One methodological warning is essential. Van Mierlo says Hadewijch’s psychology is “simple” compared with later systems such as Ruusbroec’s and unlike the more technical psychology one might associate with Eckhart. She does not give a detailed taxonomy of intellect, phantasm, species, agent intellect, possible intellect, or the “spark” of the soul. But “simple” here does not mean philosophically shallow. It means that her conceptual architecture is concentrated around a few powerful axes: soul, Reason, Love, memory, will, vision, will-conformity, and union.

2. Core vocabulary: “mind” is not a Cartesian mind

A Hadewijchian philosophy of mind must avoid imposing a post-Cartesian vocabulary. Her central word is not “mind” in the modern sense but ziel, soul. The soul includes what we would now separate into cognition, desire, affect, will, selfhood, and spiritual perception. “Mind” is therefore best used as a cautious analytic translation for the living subjectivity of the soul.

Some terms need to be kept close to the Middle Dutch.

Term Approximate sense Philosophical role ziel soul the spiritual subject as a whole; the God-visible and God-seeing being geest spirit inner/visionary state or spiritual sphere; in visions, a locus of non-bodily seeing rede reason illuminated discernment, measure, practical-intellectual judgment memorie memory not mere recall, but high thought, trust, and retention of the soul’s divine orientation wille will conative power, transformed into willing with God’s will sinne / inneghe sinne senses / inward senses lower and inner perceptual-affective powers needing guidance Minne Love divine Love, God’s Love, Christ as Beloved, the governing force of spiritual life caritate charity in Letter XVIII, the soul’s natural sight, with Love and Reason as its two eyes ghebruken fruition/enjoyment the immediate enjoyment of Love/God ghebreken lack/want/deficiency the painful condition of not yet possessing/enjoying Love fully

Van Mierlo’s commentary is especially useful here. He notes that Hadewijch’s soul has senses that can draw it toward lower, earthly, alien things, and also inward senses guided by illuminated Reason; he also emphasizes that Hadewijch does not yet possess the intricate soul-psychology of Ruusbroec.

The most important single formula appears in Letter XVIII. Hadewijch says the soul is a being “visible” or “seeable” to God, and God is visible to it. She then describes the soul as a groundless depth in which God has satisfaction in Himself, and as a free way through which God passes from His depth, while God is also the way through which the soul passes into divine depth. This is the core of her philosophy of mind.

3. The ontology of the soul: reciprocal visibility and abyssal capacity

Hadewijch’s soul is not a sealed interior substance. It is defined by reciprocal visibility: God sees the soul; the soul sees God. This already breaks with any account of mind as private, self-enclosed interiority. The soul is a subject whose essence is relation to divine sight. It is “mind” as exposure, transparency, and Godward intentionality.

This reciprocal visibility is not merely epistemic; it is ontological. The soul wants to “satisfy” God, to keep rightful dominion over itself, and to avoid falling beneath its dignity through alien or lower things. Hadewijch therefore links cognition, dignity, self-rule, and desire. A mind is well-formed when it remains in its proper nobility and is not captured by what is vreemd, alien to Love.

Her abyss language is decisive. The soul is “groundlessness,” but not emptiness in a nihilistic sense. It is inexhaustible capacity. It can be satisfied only by the whole God; anything less fails to fulfill its structure. Van Mierlo’s commentary captures the point: God and the soul are two abysses passing through one another into their depths; the soul cannot be satiated except by the whole God.

This has a striking consequence: the human mind is proportioned to the infinite, but not by being naturally infinite. Its capacity is deific and participatory. The soul is finite as creature, yet created with an orientation that no finite object can satisfy. This gives Hadewijch a strong account of intentional excess: longing is not accidental; it reveals the metaphysical structure of the soul.

Hadewijch’s union language must be handled carefully. It can sound as if the soul is simply absorbed into God, but the more careful reading is non-annihilative deification. Van Mierlo comments on Letter IX that Hadewijch describes union as mutual indwelling—mouth in mouth, heart in heart, body in body, soul in soul—while both remain themselves and do not lose their own personality. That is crucial for philosophy of mind: perfected union is not the destruction of subjectivity but its transfiguration.

4. The three faculties: Reason, Memory, Will

Hadewijch distinguishes three spiritual powers: Rede, Memorie, and Wille. Van Mierlo treats this as a classical medieval division and as an image of the Trinity. In the commentary on Letter IV, he aligns Memory with the Father, Reason with the Son, and Will with the Holy Spirit; the powers are associated with force or high thought, truth, and fervor respectively.

There is a nuance: Hadewijch’s Trinitarian mapping is not a rigid one-to-one schematic. Van Mierlo notes that elsewhere, especially Letter XXII, she seems to speak differently: God gives His nature in the soul with three powers so that the three Persons may be loved with enlightened Reason, Memory, and burning Will. Beukes also emphasizes the dialectical nature of this Trinitarian faculty structure, citing Letter XXII’s association of enlightened reason, memory, and flaming will with loving the divine Persons.

For a reconstructed philosophy of mind, the main point is not the exact mapping but the claim that the soul’s faculties are Trinitarianly patterned. The human mind is an image-structure ordered toward participation in divine life. It does not merely know God as an object; its own powers are created to correspond to, love, and be unified with the triune divine.

Letter IV gives the most vivid functional account. When Reason is darkened, the Will becomes weak and powerless, and Memory loses its “high thought” and trust. This is a psychological claim: cognitive obscuration produces motivational weakness and loss of orienting memory. Conversely, when God gives Reason light, eternity, and truth, Will receives new strength and Memory becomes bold.

Memory in Hadewijch is therefore not mere archive. It is the retention of the soul’s high orientation, its confidence, and its capacity to remain directed toward the Beloved. Will is not bare choice; it is the power to act, endure, obey, and burn toward Love. Reason is not detached calculation; it is illuminated discernment. The faculties are mutually dependent, and disorder in one cascades through the others.

Van Mierlo’s broader synthesis says that the soul must gather itself into enicheit, unity, in order to be united with divine Unity. The three powers connect the soul with the three Persons, but Love finally gathers the soul into the One. This gives Hadewijch a developmental psychology of integration: the mind’s destiny is not simply correct belief but unified deiform life.

5. Reason and Love: the two eyes of the soul

The most important Hadewijchian noetic doctrine is the two-eyed sight of the soul. Letter XVIII says that the sight naturally created in the soul is caritate, and that this sight has two eyes: Minne and Reason. Reason cannot see God except in what God is not; Love rests only in what God is. Reason proceeds toward God through what God is not, while Love sets aside what God is not and rejoices precisely where it lacks, in what God is.

This is a sophisticated epistemology. Reason is apophatic, analogical, mediated, and discriminating. It knows by negation and by created traces. Love is immediate, participatory, and acquisitive in the sense of wanting God as God is. Reason has sobriety, measure, and paths; Love has sweetness, blessedness, and the power of direct adherence. Neither is sufficient alone.

Hadewijch explicitly says they help each other: Reason teaches Love, and Love enlightens Reason. When Reason falls into Love’s desire and Love allows itself to be bound by Reason’s measure, they can accomplish an “over-great work,” but this cannot be learned without feeling or experience. That last clause is philosophically important. Some knowledge is not transmissible as doctrine alone; it requires affective participation. Hadewijch is not anti-intellectual, but she denies that purely external, non-participatory cognition is adequate to Love.

This also complicates the common reception cliché that women mystics are “affective” while men are “speculative.” A Cambridge article on Hadewijch notes that affective mysticism is often gendered in scholarship, with women linked to emotional experience and men to intellectual experience. But Hadewijch’s own doctrine resists that split: Love and Reason are co-constitutive organs of spiritual cognition.

Van Mierlo states the point directly: Hadewijch’s mysticism is not mere feeling-mysticism. It remains under the guidance of illuminated human Reason until the human passes over into the divine and God/Love takes the lead. Reason prevents degeneration into sentimentality and secures the seriousness of the rational soul’s ascent to God.

6. Error, illusion, and discernment

Hadewijch has a robust theory of mental error. The danger is not only false propositions; it is distorted spiritual judgment. In Letter IV she warns that Reason can go wrong when misunderstood or poorly followed. Van Mierlo calls the whole letter a teaching on discernment of spirits.

The remarkable feature is that Hadewijch’s examples are often apparently good things: fear of God, hope, charity, tears, devotion, sweetness, strict obedience, spiritual exercises, distinctions among works, and concern with punishment or reward. These can all mislead when detached from illuminated measure.

This yields a subtle philosophy of affective cognition. Emotions and devotions are not dismissed as irrational; they are meaningful spiritual states. But they are fallible. Fear can become servility. Sweetness can become attachment to consolation. Charity can become self-willed interference. Obedience can become unfree submission to the wrong authority. Reason’s task is not to suppress affect but to discern its truth-value in relation to Minne.

Hadewijch therefore anticipates, in a medieval theological key, something like a virtue-epistemological account of mind: the soul knows well only when its powers are rightly trained, ordered, humbled, and purified. Recent philosophical scholarship has started to notice this. Savynska’s 2024 article argues that Hadewijch’s concept of Rede has philosophical roots in Augustinian Neoplatonism, Stoic self-control, and traditions of personified Reason or Lady Philosophy as a guide to epistemic virtues.

7. Will, freedom, and selfhood

For Hadewijch, the will is perfected not by autonomous self-assertion but by willing with God. This does not mean passivity. It means that the will becomes free by losing alien self-will and becoming aligned with Minne. Van Mierlo stresses that Hadewijch’s “coming to nought” is not the loss of personality but the death of self-seeking; the soul that wants nothing but what God wants becomes deified.

Fraeters’ analysis of the visions makes this central. In the first vision Hadewijch learns that she can become God’s bride only when she is one with God, especially by bringing her will into agreement with God’s will. The transformative moment in Visions V and VI occurs when a God-alien aspect of her personality is destroyed and her will becomes one with God’s will.

This is not a denial of agency; it is a transformation of agency. In Letter XVIII, the high soul reads judgments from God’s face, acts according to the truth of Minne, obeys Minne alone, and serves Love’s works day and night “in all freedom.” Freedom is thus not indifferent choice among options. It is non-alienated action in accord with the highest object of the soul’s nature.

This also clarifies Hadewijch’s language of obedience. Obedience to Minne is the opposite of servility to alien social or psychological forces. The soul becomes free from fear of reputation, social pressure, false prudence, and externally imposed “commands” that contradict Love’s command.

8. Desire, affect, and the intentionality of longing

Hadewijch’s psychology is intensely affective, but affect is not opposed to cognition. Desire is a way of knowing lack. Pain is a way of measuring distance from Love. Sweetness is a partial sign of Love’s presence, but also a danger if mistaken for the whole. Longing is the soul’s intentional relation to the God it does not yet fully enjoy.

The pair ghebreken and ghebruken is fundamental. Ghebruken is fruition, enjoyment, or direct savoring of God/Love. Ghebreken is deficiency, lack, or the painful earthly condition of not yet possessing what the soul seeks. RozemarijnOnline’s Dutch mysticism course, discussing Vision VI, distinguishes visionary seeing from mystical unification and maps ghebruken to direct divine experience and ghebreken to the earthly/material condition of lack.

Fraeters’ reading of Vision XIV sharpens the point. The highest mystical life is not simply heavenly bliss or ecstatic enjoyment. Vision XIV corrects the illusion that the blissful unity-experience is the highest good. The full mystical life occurs on earth, in the place of deficiency, through imitation of the suffering Christ; for Hadewijch, lijden and leiden—suffering and leading—belong together.

Thus the mature mind does not escape lack by fantasy. It inhabits lack rightly. It transforms ghebreken into disciplined desire, service, and Christic conformity. Desire is therefore not merely a subjective appetite; it is a structure of spiritual intentionality, testing whether the soul seeks consolation, self, or God Himself.

9. Visionary cognition and mystical cognition

Hadewijch distinguishes several levels of cognition, even if she does not systematize them scholastically.

First, there is ordinary or moral cognition, in which Reason discerns works, motives, virtues, and dangers.

Second, there is illuminated cognition, in which Reason is given divine light and works with Love.

Third, there is visionary cognition, where truths are shown through images, angelic settings, symbolic figures, and celestial ranks.

Fourth, there is mystical fruition, where the soul passes beyond discursive knowing into direct enjoyment.

Vision VI gives the clearest example. Van Mierlo’s commentary says that Hadewijch, then nineteen, desired to know how God receives those lost in Him and taken into His enjoyment. She sees God, understands divine judgments, sees blessing and punishment, heaven and hell, and God’s paradoxical spatiality—His length below all, smallness above all, hiddenness around all, wideness within all. Then, seeing God’s enjoyment in His breast, she falls swallowed up “without knowledge” for half an hour. Van Mierlo explicitly identifies two stages: first she knows and understands God; second she is outside all knowing and cognition in blessed enjoyment.

This is a crucial philosophy-of-mind distinction. Hadewijch does not reduce knowledge to propositions. She recognizes a kind of knowing that culminates in non-knowing, not as ignorance but as trans-discursive fruition. The mind’s highest act is not conceptual mastery but participatory union.

Medieval visionary theory helps contextualize this. RozemarijnOnline summarizes a medieval view in which visions are not bodily sense perceptions, dreams, or madness, but truths shown to the soul through inner eyes, often by angelic mediation. The same source distinguishes vision, which uses images and can be recounted, from immediate mystical experience, which lacks images and is hard to describe.

This framework fits Hadewijch’s Vision VI: the image-filled vision is intelligible and narratable; the direct absorption into divine enjoyment is less discursively available. The mind therefore has both symbolic-visionary and non-symbolic-fruitive modes.

10. The body, Christ, and anti-escapist psychology

Hadewijch is not a simple body-denying dualist. She certainly warns against lower senses and alien attachments, but her highest model is Christ’s embodied humanity. The soul must live Christ’s humanity before it presumes to enjoy His divinity.

Van Mierlo’s Christological section says that for Hadewijch, following Christ is not merely external imitation but “living Christ” inwardly, taking on His spirit, and living His humanity in suffering, poverty, labor, and the cross. To live God with God, one must first want to live humanly with Christ’s humanity.

Beukes similarly argues that Hadewijch’s Minnemystik is Trinitarian and Christological rather than merely erotic or “mystical-sexual.” He emphasizes that the personification of God’s Love is the incarnate Christ in His corporeality, and that loving Christ entails the practice of virtues, participation in Christ’s mysteries, and endurance with Christ in poverty, humiliation, and suffering.

For philosophy of mind, this means that Hadewijch’s perfected soul is not one that flees embodiment into private ecstasy. The mind is formed through embodied virtue, suffering, service, and imitation of Christ. Vision XIV is especially important because it places the highest mystical vocation back on earth, in ghebreken, rather than in continuous ecstatic ghebruken.

11. The mind of God

Hadewijch does not offer a technical “philosophy of divine mind” in the scholastic sense. But she gives enough to reconstruct one respectfully. God’s “mind” in her world is not a larger version of human mentality. It is the divine life of truth, will, justice, Love, self-knowledge, and self-enjoyment, in which the human soul participates.

The vision commentary summarizes God as one, eternal, infinite, almighty, ineffable, incomprehensible, all-encompassing, and both immanent and transcendent. It also identifies a strong Trinitarian structure: the Son is born from the Father as subsistent knowledge and truth; the Holy Spirit proceeds as subsistent Love; God is one in essence and three in Persons.

God’s cognition appears in Hadewijch as divine face, divine judgment, divine will, and divine self-enjoyment. In Vision VI, Hadewijch sees in God’s face the order of human destinies, reward, punishment, wandering, return, and divine paradox. In Letter XVIII, the high soul reads judgments from God’s face and acts according to the truth of Minne’s ways.

The divine mind is therefore normative. It does not merely observe; it orders, judges, illumines, and gives the measure by which the soul’s actions become true. Hadewijch’s epistemology is not just “how humans know God” but “how human knowing is re-formed by God’s own knowing.”

God also “enjoys Himself” in the soul. In Letter XVIII, the soul is the groundlessness in which God has satisfaction in Himself, and God is the way through which the soul passes into divine ground. This is not a claim that the soul contains God as a container contains an object. It is a participatory claim: the deified soul becomes a site of divine self-presence and self-fruition.

This matters for the philosophy of mind because Hadewijch’s account of consciousness is not merely subject-object intentionality. At the highest level, the object of consciousness—God—becomes the transforming source of consciousness. The soul does not just think about God; it is drawn into God’s own life of Love, truth, and will.

12. Angelic minds

The minds of angels are also philosophically important in Hadewijch. They are not distractions. In her visionary cosmos, angels mediate, guide, structure, lift, interpret, and disclose. They belong to the epistemic ecology of the soul.

Fraeters notes that in the first vision Hadewijch contemplates God from the choir of the throne angels, the lowest contemplative angelic choir, while in the last vision she contemplates from the choir of the seraphim, closest to God. In a more specialized Dutch article, Fraeters summarizes scholarly debate about whether the angelic ascent from throne angels to seraphim is the central structuring principle of the visions; even where that thesis is contested, she notes that angels acting as guides do give sequence to the visions.

Vision XIII is the richest angelic text. It shows Hadewijch a new heaven of the seraphim, a divine Face with six wings, a great company of seraphim accompanying adorned spirits, and Hadewijch’s own seraph lifting her so that she may see in the eye of the Face.

A philosophy of angelic mind can be reconstructed cautiously from this. Angels are non-human intellectual and contemplative agents differentiated by proximity to God. Their noetic role is not the same as human Reason or Love. They mediate symbolic disclosure: they bring the soul into visionary settings, show what must be seen, interpret rank and meaning, and sometimes authorize the visionary’s vocation. Their cognition is hierarchical, liturgical, and participatory.

But angelic mediation is not the final term. Hadewijch’s highest mystical state goes beyond image, angelic scene, and narratable vision into direct divine fruition. This does not demote angels into “mere symbols.” Rather, it locates them: angelic minds mediate divine truth in forms the soul can see; direct union exceeds even that mediation.

13. Language, metaphor, and the limits of conceptual thought

Hadewijch’s philosophy of mind also includes a theory of language, or at least a practice of language that implies one. Her metaphors do not merely decorate a doctrine. They train the reader’s consciousness.

A recent French study by Isabelle Raviolo, published by Éditions de l’Université de Lorraine, argues that Hadewijch’s metaphors in the poetic language of love do not simply represent something; they lead the reader “to the edge of nothing,” using abyss, drowning, nudity, and desert imagery to enact an experience in which conceptualization is set aside and metaphor exceeds language.

This fits Hadewijch’s own epistemology. If the highest fruition cannot be learned without feeling and lies beyond the ordinary reach of wisdom’s conceptual scrutiny, then language must do more than define. It must move, wound, attract, frustrate, and transform. The mind is formed by language that presses it beyond conceptual possession toward participatory desire.

Thus Hadewijch’s metaphors are exercises of cognition. They are not irrational substitutes for thought; they are instruments for bringing the reader to the limit where Reason and Love must work together, and where Love finally leads beyond discursive knowing.

14. Social and pedagogical mind: mystagogy, not private experience

Hadewijch’s mind is not solitary. Her writings are addressed to friends, disciples, and an inner circle. Fraeters states that Hadewijch’s texts were written for an intended addressee or circle and that her visions were written down at the request of a particular friend in spiritual need.

Fraeters’ article on the visions argues that they function not merely didactically but mystagogically. The communicative situation is not a detached teacher transferring information to a passive learner. It is a formation of personality through a relation between Hadewijch, her friend, and God as a dominant third term. Hadewijch and the addressee are at different temporal stages, but from the perspective of eternity they belong to a shared movement toward unity with God.

This is important for philosophy of mind. Hadewijch’s soul is formed in community. Spiritual knowledge is not merely private introspection but guided transformation. The mature mind becomes a path for others, just as Christ is a path for Hadewijch. Fraeters explicitly notes that Hadewijch’s growth to God-formed perfection functions as an exemplum for her addressee.

Mind, then, is also pedagogical and intersubjective. The soul receives, is guided, writes, exhorts, leads, suffers for others, and becomes a means of another soul’s transformation.

15. A reconstructed Hadewijchian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive extraction can be stated in twelve theses.

First, the mind is the soul as a whole spiritual subject. It includes cognition, affect, memory, will, longing, perception, selfhood, and capacity for divine participation.

Second, the soul is relationally constituted. It is “seeable” to God and capable of seeing God. Its deepest structure is reciprocal visibility, not self-enclosed privacy.

Third, the mind is abyssal capacity. It cannot be satisfied by finite goods because it is proportioned, by grace and creation, toward the whole God.

Fourth, the mind is Trinitarianly patterned. Reason, Memory, and Will are spiritual powers through which the soul corresponds to, loves, and participates in the divine Persons, though Hadewijch’s mapping of powers to Persons is flexible rather than mechanically fixed.

Fifth, Reason and Love are co-essential epistemic powers. Reason discerns, measures, negates, and guides; Love adheres, tastes, suffers lack, and reaches toward God as God is. The soul sees with both eyes.

Sixth, affect is cognitive. Longing, sweetness, fear, pain, and lack are not mere subjective moods; they disclose the soul’s relation to Love. But they are fallible and require discernment.

Seventh, mental error is often misdirected good. Devotion, obedience, charity, fear, and spiritual sweetness can become false when detached from illuminated Reason and Minne’s order.

Eighth, freedom is transformed willing. The will becomes free not by arbitrary autonomy but by willing with God’s will and obeying Minne alone.

Ninth, the highest knowledge exceeds discursive knowledge. Vision VI shows a movement from seeing and understanding God to being swallowed into divine enjoyment without ordinary knowing.

Tenth, the body and earthly life matter. The perfected soul does not flee the world into permanent ecstasy; it returns to embodied Christic suffering, virtue, and service.

Eleventh, God’s mind and angelic minds are integral. God is the archetypal truth, will, judgment, and Love in which the human mind participates. Angels are real noetic mediators in the visionary order, guiding the soul through symbolic knowledge toward direct divine fruition.

Twelfth, selfhood is not annihilated by union. The soul’s deification is a mutual indwelling in which the soul becomes Godlike while retaining personal distinction.

16. What Hadewijch contributes to philosophy of mind

Hadewijch’s contribution is not a theory of mental representation, nor a metaphysics of consciousness in modern analytic form. It is a participatory, transformative, Trinitarian psychology of the soul.

She gives a model in which mind is not separable from love, will, and formation. Cognition is not merely seeing-that but becoming-fit-to-see. Desire is not a brute appetite but the soul’s intentional stretch toward its only adequate object. Reason is not the enemy of mystical experience but its guide and purifier. Love is not irrational feeling but a higher mode of participation that enlightens Reason. Memory is not storage but fidelity to the soul’s divine horizon. Will is not free because it can choose anything, but because it becomes free from alien motives and capable of willing with God.

Her account also widens philosophy of mind beyond the human. God’s mind is not a theological distraction but the source and norm of human mindedness. Angelic minds are not ornamental but mediating intelligences in the visionary economy. The human soul’s cognition is embedded in a hierarchy of divine, angelic, communal, embodied, and affective relations.

The most precise label for the system might be: a deific, affective-rational, relational philosophy of the soul. It is deific because the mind’s fulfillment is Godlikeness. It is affective-rational because Love and Reason must work together. It is relational because the soul is constituted by visibility to God, guidance by angels and teachers, and service to other souls. It is a philosophy of mind in which the highest act of mind is not detached contemplation but transformed participation in Love.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
